title: Support redirecting pushes to registries
body:
### Feature request description

Hi,

podman push/pull requires to give a precise host and port as part of the image name or configured in registries.conf. 

Would be nice and useful, if just a domain name could be given (or even a placeholder for the default domain), and the host(s) and port(s) to pull from or push to be found by DNS, maybe only if a special option like --use-srv is given. 

Currently, if the admin moves a local company registry to a different machine/port, pulling can be updated through registries.conf, but pushing requires changes in all scripts/makefiles/receipts. 

regards


### Suggest potential solution

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body:
Hi @hadmut, 

This is a cool idea to use SRV DNS records, I'm not sure if it's complexity we would like to introduce though.

We may consider adding support for `image pull` to use the same location override in registries.conf that `image pull` uses today but we will need a maintainer to weigh in on that. 

> The user-specified image reference is, primarily, a "logical" image name, always used for naming the image. By default, the image reference also directly specifies the registry and repository to use, but the following options can be used to redirect the underlying accesses to different registry servers or locations (e.g. to support configurations with no access to the internet without having to change Dockerfiles, or to add redundancy).
> 
> location : Accepts the same format as the prefix field, and specifies the physical location of the prefix-rooted namespace.
> 
> By default, this is equal to prefix (in which case prefix can be omitted and the [[registry]] TOML table can only specify location).
> 
> Example: Given
> 
> prefix = "example.com/foo"
> location = "internal-registry-for-example.com/bar"
> 
> requests for the image example.com/foo/myimage:latest will actually work with the internal-registry-for-example.com/bar/myimage:latest image.

c/image handles pulling/pushing so I move the issue there
cc @mtrmac 


> Currently, if the admin moves a local company registry to a different machine/port, pulling can be updated through registries.conf, but pushing requires changes in all scripts/makefiles/receipts.

It is no clear why push is different? registries.conf should apply to pull and push equally.

In any case I don't think it makes sense for podman to invent some new SRV record for specifying registry hosts/ports. If there should be a standard it should likely be proposed tot he distribution spec so all runtimes will implement it. https://github.com/opencontainers/distribution-spec

Regardless a registry is just a normal HTTP/HTTPS server so normal SVCB/HTTPS records would suffice I think, I guess it would only come down to specifying that clients should use them?

What @Luap99 says: a network-visible feature should probably have an interoperable cross-vendor spec; that’s possible but I think somewhat unlikely, or at least not something I’m likely to be working on.

Adding a `registries.conf` option that would redirect pushes is something we can do locally in c/image / Podman etc., and I think that would make sense.

Especially if the original proposal says
> maybe only if a special option like --use-srv is given.

i.e. adjustments on the computer that is making the push are _already_ assumed, changing `registries.conf` would be one local change and it would not _require_ adding a DNS record — `registries.conf` would be simpler all around. (OTOH it would still be _possible_ to build `registries.conf` based on some site-specific DNS records if that were the local preference.)

srv records are state of the art and a commonly accepted method of LAN administation. 

In contrast, registries.conf is proprietery, and requires cooperation of all administrators of all machines. If the registry is moved, it requires to inform every single user (=admin of his own machine) to update his/her registries.conf, which is a lot of overhead and error prone. 

Furthermore, registries.conf does not support using different local servers when e.g. in different branches of the same company. 

And: SRV records do support (similar to MX records) to use multiple (=redundant) servers.
